data.title: Fairfax Financial: Still Undervalued Despite A 400% Surge
data.description:
When I started buying Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited (FRFHF) in the second half of 2025, the stock had already climbed more than 400% over the last five years. It outperformed not just its peers but all indices by a wide margin.

I know many of you might not have even heard about this company, and that's fair. This is a Canadian company, not much followed. It's not a fancy tech, space, or drone manufacturer but a good old P&C (Property & Casualty) insurance business that is still trading at valuations that I find very attractive.

Thesis

At a price-to-earnings multiple of 8x, I see Fairfax as extremely undervalued compared to other companies in its space, like Intact Financial (IFC:CA) and Markel (MKL). It has been consistently achieving a 15%+ Return on Equity in addition to a combined ratio near or below 95, which is a combination that you don't see every day in a company trading at this valuation.

On top of that, as if trading at a price-to-book (P/B) multiple near 1.47x for a business that aims to grow its book by 15% every year and has actually been doing it successfully isn't cheap enough, there is roughly $117 per share hidden value (later in this article) that is not included in the book value today. Adding it increases the BVPS from ~$1203 to $1320, dropping the P/B multiple to 1.29x.

At a midpoint of 12% EPS growth from a combination of interest income, insurance premium income, equity gains, and share buybacks, I see a 42% upside from the current price with a price target of around $2400 for the next 12-18 months.

Business Overview

In very basic terms, I see Fairfax as a holding company that is in the business of writing Property & Casualty insurance to generate float, which it can then invest and generate returns on, much like Berkshire Hathaway. On the insurance side, its goal is to maintain a low combined ratio. This essentially means getting paid to hold the float. Even though Fairfax, the main company, is Canadian, the operations are diversified across North America, Asia, Africa, and South America, and it owns some of the insurers you might have heard about, like Northbridge, Odyssey Group, Allied World, and Crum & Forster, among others.

Unlike other companies, where acquirers usually look for synergies and consolidation after an acquisition, Fairfax runs on a decentralized basis for everything except investment decisions.

Decentralization is the best system for unleashing the power of the many, rather than being limited to the talents of the few. And it aligns so perfectly with the foundational values of Fairfax since its inception. Our optimism in what empowered people can accomplish is unbounded! - Prem Watsa, Chairman, Fairfax

Another pillar of Fairfax's strength is its investment portfolio, which is valued at approximately $70.9 billion.

In Q3 2025 alone, the net gains on its portfolio were $426.2 million. Not only that, but Fairfax's underwriting profits also went up 38.64%, from $389.7 million to $540.3 million.

So the company is doing well on both fronts: underwriting profits and investment gains.

Past Problems

In 2021 and for a few years before that, Fairfax was trading at multiples below its book value, and I'm not reluctant to say that the market was not wrong in doing so. The company did make some massive mistakes in the past, like shorting the broader market between 2010 and 2016.

But this wasn't the only macro-bet that the company made. Before that, Fairfax had a short position in the 2008 GFC. But the difference is that while the company cashed in about $4 billion from this one, the next bet (from 2010 to 2016) cost it billions, and the company missed the chance to ride a bull market.

Even though it did remove these hedges, the aftertaste lasted a long time. While the company kept reiterating in its annual letters for the next several years that it wouldn't be hedging against the market again and that it had changed its investment strategy, the market still wasn't ready to change its mind about Fairfax.

However, after 2021, 2022, things started to change. As Fairfax started posting positive results, both in underwriting and in its portfolio, and as Prem Watsa (Fairfax Chairman) proved that he could indeed stay away from making macro bets, it started to regain some of the confidence that it lost due to its past actions. And this is reflected in the way the P/B ratio has changed since 2021, from 0.9x to 1.47x today.

Valuation

In order to fairly assess Fairfax's valuation, I will be using a normalized EPS of $180.55 instead of the TTM EPS of $206 so that we can avoid the peak from skewing our results as we approach a soft insurance market. Current stock price is $1,710.

To do that, I have used the BVPS of $1,203.65 (as of Sep 30, 2025) and the long-term average ROE of 15%. I will assume a 10x P/E.

Now, my goal isn't to be microscopic here, because if a decimal-point change can turn our thesis from undervalued to overvalued, there isn't really much margin of safety. So, my goal here is to have an estimated range to see if buying at current prices makes sense for a long position.

As you can see in the table above, even in the most conservative scenario where the EPS grows by just 9% annually over the next five years and we expect an 18% return, the stock is still trading below its fair value by about 11%.

However, I think a better approach would be to take the midpoint of 12% growth and a 15% discount rate, and in that scenario, we have an upside of 42%.

Comparative Valuation

For the valuation of Fairfax, I used a P/E ratio of 10 because if you look at the chart below, despite having a higher Return on Equity, it is still trading at multiples that are lower than its peers'. So, a 10x price-to-earnings multiple is still a reasonable assumption.

Now, if you are familiar with insurance industry metrics, you might say that the lower multiple might be because of Fairfax's underwriting or combined ratio. But that doesn't seem to be the case here. Fairfax has been consistently posting a combined ratio that is on par with the top industry players.

Dividend and Buybacks

Fairfax does pay a dividend, but the yield is less than 1%. This is because Fairfax has been returning capital to shareholders through buybacks instead of paying a higher dividend, as you can see in the chart below. And the company indeed bought a lot of stock last year.

Fairfax also owns TRS (total return swaps) for its own stock to embellish shareholder returns without tying up too much capital, as would be the case if it just bought that much stock in the open market.

Looking Forward

Looking forward, Fairfax's income streams are quite diversified. It stands to benefit from its global insurance business, high yield on its bonds, and equity gains. In 2024, the company earned around $2.5 billion in interest and dividends, and for 2025, this income is expected to be near this number as well. Not only that, the company has reiterated that it can sustain this yield for the foreseeable future, which will provide stable cash flows for investments.

Another thing that I want to add is that Fairfax isn't a company that will chase growth at any cost or prioritize short-term over long-term. Fairfax hasn't shied away from writing unprofitable policies in the past. While this may affect short-term earnings, I see this discipline helping the company in maintaining, if not improving, its current combined ratio.

Our operating income will give us a lot of stability going forward but realized gains on common stocks, bonds and other investments, even though lumpy, will be significant over the long term. - Prem Watsa

(If you are interested in an in-depth portfolio analysis of Fairfax, please refer to this article on Seeking Alpha.)

The Hidden Value

The $117 in hidden value that I mentioned earlier comes from the way the company accounts for its investments. Many of Fairfax's investments in common stocks and consolidated companies are equity accounted rather than mark-to-market. Put another way, these investments are significantly worth more than their carrying value.

At the end of 2024, this 'surplus' was around $1.5 billion. On Sep 30, 2025, this surplus was worth $2.5 billion (+66% in nine months) or $117/share. This surplus isn't added to the book value at present, but as the company starts to realize these investments, the book value increases with it.

Risks

When it comes to Fairfax, there are two major risks that I see apart from general macroeconomic risks.

In a soft insurance market, we can reasonably expect that Fairfax's insurance business would see a slowdown in growth. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but it is still something worth considering. The company has said multiple times that its priority is profitability over gaining unprofitable market share. While total premiums may go down, I expect the combined ratio to stay at current levels (<95), unless there is a catastrophic event that causes it to go higher, which is another risk to consider.

On the investment side, Fairfax has benefited tremendously from high interest in its bond portfolio in recent years, but as we see interest rates stabilize, the portfolio performance will be more dependent on equities and their performance. Since many of Fairfax's investments are international, like Eurobank and Fairfax India, exchange rates can also have a noticeable impact.

The Bottom Line

To sum up, even though I don't necessarily expect Fairfax to repeat its last five-year returns over the next five years, I do expect that the company will continue to outperform the market and its peers.

And this confidence stems from the fact that, despite having a high-teens ROE and profitable underwriting, Fairfax is still trading at multiples that are lower than what it deserves based on its business and portfolio performance and peer comparisons. I see the multiples expanding as the market starts to rethink Fairfax's strategy and its valuation.

I therefore assign a STRONG BUY rating to the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy Microsoft's Falling Price Despite An Overvalued Macroeconomy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Microsoft (MSFT) analysis, in which I issued a conservative Hold rating, the stock has declined by -18% in price. The stock is down over -20% from its all-time high. As a result, I recently initiated a starter tranche, currently holding the position at about 2.5% of NAV, with plans to scale on any further, prolonged weakness that may emerge.

The question on every Microsoft investor's mind amid the recent sell-off is whether AI is making the business model more capital-intensive without bringing an adequate rise in long-term cash flows. Furthermore, is AI demand and pricing power already deteriorating?

Investment Position

At the last earnings report, Microsoft noted that the gross margin percentage decreased slightly due to AI infrastructure investment and rising AI usage. It's not surprising, given that capital expenditures were $37.5B in the quarter, with roughly two-thirds of that going to short-lived assets, primarily GPUs and CPUs. Even so, cash flow from operations was up by +60% year-over-year, to $35.8B, which was driven by strong cloud billings and collections. Even amid such a heavy AI spending environment, management has been trying to take care of shareholders; even though free cash flow of $5.9B was down sequentially and significantly stunted from AI capex over long-term history, $12.7B was returned via dividends and repurchases in the quarter, which is a +32% year-over-year growth in capital return.

Evidently, cash flows are being pushed out by AI infrastructure buildout, but the company is also developing a remarkably strong moat that is being unfairly discounted amid the present "SaaS-fragility" narrative.

In my opinion, MSFT's sell-off is a valuation reset hinged on AI cost absorption and growth normalization instead of deteriorating demand. MSFT may therefore very well be an anchor for the broader software multiple repricing; I don't see any significant long-term structural weakness here.

Commercial bookings are up +230% year-over-year, driven by the previously announced large OpenAI Azure commitment plus an Anthropic commitment, alongside core growth. Commercial RPO is up +110% year-over-year, with a weighted average of 2.5 years; around 25% will be recognized over the next 12 months, and about 45% of commercial RPO is from OpenAI. Microsoft's management itself has warned of something I have been alerting my readers to recently, which is that OpenAI contracts represent long-term demand needs and will drive quarterly volatility in booking and RPO growth rates as a result.

Value Opportunity

MSFT currently has a 14-week RSI value of about 35; it had breached below 30 a few days before the writing of this analysis. This is good value, though it is clearly not deeply undervalued even though the RSI shows that the sell-off has been fast and brutal (indicating it will also likely be a fast recovery).

It's wise to recognize here that while the technical indicators show a strong medium-term value trade setup, MSFT's present valuation is not de facto significantly undervalued. Rather, there is a moderate undervaluation that has occurred, and I would prefer to see a further drop of -20% or so in the P/S multiple before raising my stake to >5% of my portfolio's NAV. This would align the P/S with values seen around 2022/2023.

The most reasonable base-case outcome here is management reporting normalized EPS of $22 in June 2028, with the forward P/E stabilizing at 25 in June 2027, implying an 18-month return of +37.5% from $400 per share today to $550.

In the bear case, further incremental bleeding could occur if macro valuations sell off following the already deflated SaaS names. This is a meaningful probability outcome, but in such a macro reset it is also plausible that MSFT stock consolidates around $375–425 per share until the indices recover.

In the bull case, we get a quick rebound in MSFT stock in the next few months, hitting my $550/share target early. However, this would require prolonged consolidation in indices so that when energy constraints become clearer, capex is already in a state of quasi-digestion. At the moment, this is not happening, as hyperscalers continue to ramp up AI capex (Amazon (AMZN) $200B per annum forecasted, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) closing in on $200B per annum forecasted). I don't see how we aren't entering a capex cliff here, with the major transition catalyst being energy.

Further Risk Analysis

Structurally, Microsoft's bear case could materialize fundamentally as more capital-intensive and energy-intensive without a commensurate lift in durable pricing power. Even with hardware efficiency gains, deployment and inference at scale can remain expensive and could worsen with model complexity, reinforcing the notion of strong demand with disappointing profit conversion. Microsoft, like other hyperscalers, has entered a "capex treadmill," whereby semiconductor refresh cycles are emerging as "table stakes," making ROIC harder to defend even with top-line health.

This means that model developers may end up with a moat in the initial stages of the autonomous-technology supercycle, with the opportunity to develop much higher incremental margins as more human jobs are replaced. However, this comes with its own set of macroeconomic challenges that could affect top-line demand.

Focusing back on stock price dynamics, in extended de-rating cycles, RSI can stay low if fundamentals begin to accurately reflect the new lower valuation regime. Therefore, my Buy rating for this analysis could logically come under stress with operational inhibitions that stunt growth amid a macro valuation reset. That is why I have started MSFT at 2.5% of NAV and will scale into either weakness or strength.

Conclusion: Buy

After a significant correction from its all-time high, MSFT stock offers a tactical entry point. Investor focus is logically converging around capital intensity, energy constraints, and whether AI will enhance ROIC in the medium term, i.e., a few years, or over the longer term, i.e., decades. The company's operating cash flow is strong, and the bookings strength confirms real enterprise commitment that looks both durable and reliable. However, margin pressure in the near to medium term is a real risk, as accelerated infrastructure spending and short-lived assets are structurally altering the earnings trajectory. At this stage, we can be certain that MSFT's moat in tech will be unassailable by almost all others, except peer Mag-7 data-center behemoths.

The recent stock price setback reflects a reset in short-term expectations rather than structural decay, and in my opinion it is a buying opportunity. There's moderate upside potential in just a few months in the bull case, and in the bear case amid a broader macro valuation de-rating, a tranching strategy will allow you to average down into deeper value in this de facto leader in the long-term AI supercycle.

data.title: Boeing's Profit Illusion: The Real Turnaround Starts Now
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) stock has had a good run since my analysis discussing Q3 2025 results, gaining nearly 25%, while the S&P 500 gained only 1.6%. In my view, Boeing is currently laying the foundation, and most of that occurred in 2025, but a lot of work remains to be done over the next 12-24 months or so. In this report, I provide a detailed analysis of the company’s Q4 earnings. As I already shared in a comment to Seeking Alpha news, I wasn’t impressed with earnings, and some strategic decisions also put some pressure on the business.

Boeing Profit Driven By Divesture

Boeing’s revenues surged 57% while GAAP operating income swung from a $3.77 billion loss to an $8.8 billion profit with earnings per share rising from a $5.46 loss to a $10.23 billion profit. Non-GAAP earnings rose from a $4 billion loss to an $8.5 billion profit, or a swing per share from -$5.90 to $9.92. However, profits were driven by the divestiture of Digital Aviation Solutions, adding $11.83 in earnings per share. Absent the divestiture, Boeing remained loss-making. So, the story of the fourth quarter was not one of going from a loss to a profit from an operational perspective, but going from an operational loss to a smaller operational loss.

Boeing Commercial Airplanes Progress Masked By Boeing 777X Charge

Boeing deliveries were up substantially during the quarter, with deliveries increasing from 57 to 160. This increase was driven by increasing production rates this year and the work stoppage in Q4 last year at Boeing Commercial Airplanes. I injected the delivery mix into my model, which shows that Boeing missed estimates on deliveries by $48 million, and I expected an operating loss of $553 million. So, Boeing seems to have missed my estimates, and this was driven by a higher proportion of general expenses being attributable to BCA.

Boeing Defense, Space & Security Disappoints… Again

For the fourth quarter, I had modeled $7.33 billion in sales at a 2% margin. We see that Boeing beat by $87 million on the revenues. However, instead of reporting a $147 million profit, they reported a $507 million loss driven by a $565 million reach-forward loss on the tanker program. Even when we add this back, the margin would have been only 0.8%. One reason why the charge was required is the Spirit AeroSystems acquisition. That acquisition was needed to improve the supply chain health for the Boeing 737 program. We have seen some analysts commenting on the Spirit AeroSystems acquisitions through the scope of cost savings down the road. The charge on the Boeing 767 shows one often-overlooked fact, and that is that by buying Spirit AeroSystems, Boeing is also bringing some of the risks in-house that it had transferred to suppliers, including Spirit, years ago.

Boeing Global Services Strong Sales Growth, Margins Disappoint

Boeing Global Services revenues increased 2% and were just $41 million below my expectations. The adjusted margins were 18.8%, compared to my expectations of 18.5%. So, adjusted earnings were around $7 million better than I had anticipated.

A Big Miss for Boeing

If we put everything together, Boeing did miss my estimates by $33 million, fully covered by unallocated items being higher than expected. However, we do note that Commercial Airplane revenues were around $48 million lower than expected, while Global Services missed expectations by $41 million, and this was offset by $88 million in higher revenues for the Defense segment. Total segment operating profits of $9.405 billion missed estimates by $769 million, driven by the KC-46A tanker charge and higher general expenses for Boeing Commercial Airplanes.

The company ended the year with $29.4 billion in cash compared to $23 billion in the prior quarter, while consolidated debt increased from $53.4 billion to $54.1 billion. The net debt increased from $30.4 billion to $24.7 billion, primarily driven by the divestiture of the Aviation services business. A slight positive in an otherwise disappointing quarter was that operating cash flow was positive at $1.33 billion and free cash flow was also positive at $375 million.

Boeing Paces Well On Certification And Production

Focusing a bit more on Boeing airplane programs, there were some signs of good news. On the Boeing 777X program, Boeing has been approved for the next phase of certification flights. As part of this TIA3 phase, avionics, environmental control systems, and the auxiliary power units will be tested.

At the same time, a durability issue on GE’s GE9X engines was detected, but no impact is expected on the first deliveries expected in 2027. In the year prior to deliveries, typically cash outflows start to increase. The net cash outflow for the Boeing 777X will hit somewhat harder, as years of delays also meant that pre-delivery payments that would typically be made at this point have already been received in prior years. The company expects to be cash positive on this program by 2029.

On the Boeing 737 MAX, the production rate has increased from 38 per month to 42 per month, with another rate hike expected later this year to 47. The next increase to the production rate would be to 52, which could happen either very late this year or early next year. I believe that this will be a more critical step up in production. Prior to the Boeing 737 MAX crisis, Boeing struggled with increasing the production to more than 50 jets a month. So, this will be a test, and perhaps Boeing’s fourth line could be somewhat of a relief to the other three lines. So, I believe that while Boeing does not necessarily need the line until later this year for dedicated Boeing 737 MAX 10 production, it should consider getting the line set up in the coming months to play a role in the production rate increase from 42 to 47 or from 47 to 52.

The company still expects MAX 7 and MAX 10 certification this year and currently has 35 MAX 7/10 airplanes in inventory and just one MAX 8 left in inventory. So, the Boeing 737 MAX inventory is largely cleared, but it also means that Boeing now fully relies on production output.

On the Boeing 787 program, Boeing has five airplanes left to deliver from inventory and is currently stabilizing production at a rate of 8 airplanes per month, with a step up expected to 10 airplanes per month. Currently, the main bottleneck for the program remains seats and seat certification.

For Boeing, 2025 was a year in which it largely cleared Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 787 inventories, with only a few airplanes left to deliver this year. The company also laid the foundation for production rate increases, which were already initiated last year. Certification is pacing well, but we note that due to the timing mismatch of the Boeing 777X service entry and the pre-delivery payment receipts from customers, the cash outflow for the 777X will be larger than it would usually be. We also note that the Boeing 777X, initially launched in 2013, was expected to be in service by the end of 2019 in an optimistic scenario. The program is now set to turn cash positive on a unit basis 10 years later.

Boeing is also still liquidating customer considerations from the Boeing 737 MAX program, with $383 million left to liquidate, which mostly will be in the form of lower cash payments made upon delivery. So, there is some cash drag from the Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 777X this year. We also note that the Spirit AeroSystems business will improve as Boeing 737 MAX output rises, but on many other programs Boeing had transferred risk to the supply chain, including Spirit AeroSystems, and the company now has to address that in-house. The Defense business remains a wildcard in Boeing’s business, and we have not seen a clear turnaround there. The KC-46A remains a cost risk, and we also note that on the VC-25B program, Boeing is working on new contract terms, including a reset to the schedule, which I believe may trigger additional reach-forward losses.

What Is Boeing Stock Worth?

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The interesting story for Boeing will be how well the stock price paces with the recovery. What we see is that the current price does not leave any upside against 2027 earnings. So, normally this would be a hold rating. However, we do note that this is also a turnaround that is finally getting traction, and so selling the stock now, in my view, would not be prudent, as the trajectory for Boeing would indicate 38% with a $327.84 price target for 2028, and that is the first year where we should be able to see Boeing doing $10 billion in free cash flow while cash and debt profiles will still not have fully normalized, meaning that there is additional upside from 2028 onward.

EBITDA for 2025 came in substantially higher than previously modeled, and this was driven by divestiture activity that led to EBITDA swinging to $6.2 billion, whereas a $1.9 billion loss was expected. However, at the same time, it should be noted that cumulated EBITDA between 2026 and 2027 has been revised down by around 14%. Similarly, for free cash flow, we are seeing a 19% downward reduction, and that is driven by the cash pressure from the Boeing 777X program and the fact that the absorption of Spirit AeroSystems does not only bring elements where the margins that Spirit would normally have can be eliminated, but it brings a bigger cash drag that Spirit was carrying on wide-body programs, and Boeing has to absorb those for the moment and ultimately eliminate those as well where possible. That is also one of the reasons why we see that Boeing started turning around in 2025, but the bottom for the fundamentally supported share price is in 2026.

If we look at the financial performance, we note that margins seemingly will dip in 2026, and that is not necessarily a bad thing. The stronger performance in 2025 was largely driven by the divestiture activity, and the performance in 2026 is driven by operational performance improving for the full year. From there, we should see margins improving as well as better cash collection as capital intensity also normalizes relative to operating cash flow. Analysts are expecting that Boeing will start paying a dividend in 2027. I have also modeled that into the assessment, but I do believe that for Boeing it would be better to start returning value to shareholders once the net debt position is set to reach 0, and I believe that this could occur in 2029. So, I do believe that the company should consider returning value to shareholders starting in 2030 and perhaps in 2029, but not in 2027. In terms of debt, we should see a substantial decrease in debt this year. That decrease is not fully carried by free cash flow generation, so we will see the cash position decline somewhat, but I also believe that the company’s cash cushion is sufficient to absorb this. In 2027, we will see the debt come down further but better supported by cash flow, and by 2028, we should see the company’s cash surge substantially. So, Boeing also has some space to accelerate debt repayments by then, and the company could also elect to refinance debt at lower interest rates. The net debt leverage remains elevated in 2026, but in the two years after, we should see more acceptable net debt leverage levels.

Conclusion: Boeing Is Finally Getting Back On Track, But Earnings Were Not Great

I believe that Boeing’s Q4 earnings were not great, driven by the reach-forward loss on the KC-46A program, and the Defense business overall still looked extremely weak on an adjusted basis. The commercial business as well as the services business are looking better, with production increasing and certification pacing relatively well. At the same time, there is some headwind from Boeing 777X cash burn and integration of Spirit this year. So, we see a lot of improvements that were initiated in 2025, with some headwinds still present this year. I remain bullish on Boeing, and I believe that 2028, which is the year in which we should see the first signs of more normalized cash flow levels, shows that there is growth and value in the years beyond.

data.title: Alpha And Omega Semiconductor: Cheaper Stock, Unproven Earnings Durability
data.description:
Alpha and Omega Semiconductor Limited (AOSL) has released two consecutive sets of quarterly results since I covered the company back in October. In my view, that is enough time for me to look back on those results and see what they tell us about the business and its trajectory.

Before I get to analyzing them, I will first recap what I thought about the company at the time. It had a healthy balance sheet after selling part of its Chongqing JV facility, liquidity was solid, and management was doing well on the capital allocation and operating efficiency fronts.

But I also made the point that none of those things were enough to justify a Buy, and the stock has dipped by 25% in the intervening period, so it looks like the market agrees with my views. My position was that consistent margin improvement was key to improving the company’s narrative, and that hasn’t happened. In fact, AOSL has recorded declining topline numbers and margins from Q1 to Q2, and while I think the business can start building a stable earnings base in the near term, I’m recommending a Hold on the stock for now.

Q1–Q2 FY26: The Quarter-To-Quarter Reality

I think the cleanest way to read the last two quarters is to put the numbers side by side and let them speak first.

Metric

Q1 FY26

Q2 FY26

Revenue

$182.5M

$162.3M

Gross margin (GAAP)

~23.5%

~21.5%

Operating loss

$(4.6)M

$(13.6)M

Net loss

$(2.1)M

$(13.3)M

EPS (GAAP)

$(0.07)

$(0.45)

Inventory days

~124

~140

Cash & equivalents

~$223M

~$196M

Q1 looked like it was a stabilization quarter. Most of the watchpoints I highlighted in October came in as expected: revenue rose both sequentially and year over year, gross margin was steady in the 23% range, and it was even profitable on a non-GAAP basis. Overall, I’d have called it a quarter of progress, at least before looking at Q2’s report.

Q2 was not great, in my opinion, even though management says it’s to be expected, and the numbers were higher than expected. Still, revenue rolled over much more than I expected, the gross margin slipped back to ~21%, and the business lost $13.3 million, an $11 million increase from Q1’s $2.1 million net loss. Its inventory days also rose back up to 140 after falling to 126 in Q4 FY25 and 124 in Q1 FY26. That tells me that utilization is still a bit far from where it needs to be, and the company’s overall structure is very sensitive to swings in volume.

After listening to the earnings calls for both quarters, I’d say that the message from management is as expected. The business is struggling with seasonality, mix, utilization, and timing. It almost feels like Q1 was a temporary pause before the next downtick, instead of the start of an upward trend that I hoped for. I don’t think that’s the case, but it’s not easy to see the upside just yet.

At this stage, I’m seeing a business that is cleaner and better funded than it was a year ago, but it’s not consistent enough for my position to change.

Chongqing, My Q1 Expectations, and What Actually Played Out

I don’t think the issue at Alpha and Omega is a lack of direction. If anything, the strategy is clearer today than it was a year ago. Management is deliberately pushing the business toward higher-value products, leaning into power ICs and more complex MOSFET designs, and reinvesting aggressively in R&D now that the balance sheet has breathing room after the JV sale.

Specifically looking at the Chongqing sale, I pointed out that it allows AOSL to reset how it allocates capital and reduce its exposure to manufacturing-heavy economics. The company has freed up a large amount of cash, simplified its balance sheet, and given management more room to fund R&D and product transitions without worrying about liquidity. It already plans to spend around 25% more on R&D in calendar year 2026, so we have to wait and see what comes out of that increased expense.

On paper, I’d say that it makes sense. The AI narrative has also evolved into something more realistic because AOSL isn’t leaning so much on direct GPU VRM exposure. That exposure is turning out to be smaller than initially hoped, so the company is now positioning itself further upstream in power conversion. We can see that with the way management is speaking about its plans for medium-voltage MOSFETs, which still benefit from data center and AI-driven power demand. That’s a more credible angle, and I give them credit for adjusting rather than forcing a weak story.

For me, the main bottleneck is execution showing up in the income statement. R&D spend is rising at the same time that margins are falling. Its product mix is improving conceptually, but gross margins are down, and the business is struggling with cyclicality tied to utilization and volume. Taken together, the operating leverage works against AOSL quickly when demand softens even slightly.

I understand that this is a strategy for the long term, and I have faith in it helping the company become structurally better over time. At the same time, I cannot ignore the current reality around execution, where the improvements haven’t proved durable enough to smooth out the volatility that is present every quarter.

The Stock is Cheaper, but the Fundamentals Still Don’t Earn a Buy

Back in my October write-up, my core message was simple: I needed to see margin stability and repeatability before I’d get more constructive. Today, the stock is materially cheaper than it was then, but the operating picture still isn’t stable enough for me to upgrade.

Metric

Today (post Q2 FY26)

Share price

~$909M

~(40M)
