Above, I’ve compared the company’s value in October 2025 against today, mid-February 2026. What stands out to me is that the valuation now represents a stock that is priced as if it has a lot to prove, which is true.

It is cheaper today, but it is not compelling just yet. We’ve seen the gross margin bottom out, and I need it to get back to the upper mid-20s range and stay there for a few quarters first. That will only happen if the topline grows faster than we’ve seen so far in H1 FY26, and I’m unwilling to stick my neck out and say that said growth is already on the horizon.

I don’t consider valuation as a headwind at this point. Conversely, it also isn’t a tailwind on its own just because the stock is cheap. In my opinion, it is fairly priced for a business that is trying to achieve some level of earnings durability.

What Would Trigger a Rating Upgrade for Me

Management is confident that the Q2 performance was a one-off. I’m not too convinced about that, but we’ll have to wait and see. Again, the main thing I’m watching over the next few quarters is consistency with the numbers.

Gross margins need to get back to Q1’s levels or even higher, and they ideally should stay there for several consecutive quarters. Given that management is devoting 25% more money to R&D in 2026 than they did in calendar 2025, I don’t see how that will happen without some major improvements in the product mix and topline.

Operating leverage also needs to keep trending in the right direction. Sustaining bigger losses is intolerable at this point, given all the divestments and renewed focus on core business areas that management is preaching. I don’t think it is sustainable for AOSL’s cost structure to keep breaking down whenever it has to deal with softer demand and volume. Revenue should increase moving forward, and I expect those operating losses to be even narrower when that happens.

Final Thoughts

All things considered, AOSL is a better-run company today than it was a year ago, but it still needs to work on several things to build investor confidence in its fundamentals and trajectory. I don’t believe that the company’s near-term financial profile supports a re-rating for the stock price, so a Hold is the reasonable option at this point.

data.title: United Natural Foods: Staying Patient
data.description:
Shares of food distributor United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI) have been somewhat volatile a quarter after rallying in early October 2025 due to a much stronger-than-anticipated FY26 outlook due to efficiency gains. That outlook was maintained in December 2025—when the company reported 1QFY26 financials in December—and followed by wholly achievable FY28 objectives revealed at its Investor Day. With net leverage projected to fall from 4.2 in 1QFY25 to sub 2.0 by YEFY27 while trading at approximately 14.4 times FY27E EPS with tepid Street support, the recent insider buying in UNFI merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

United Natural Foods, Inc. is a Providence, Rhode Island-based distributor of ~230,000 grocery and non-food products from nearly 10,000 suppliers to all 50 states and Canada. The company, known as UNFI, services its 30,000+ customer locations from 49 distribution centers and warehouses comprising ~28 million square feet. With roots dating back to 1976, UNFI was formed through the merger of regional distributors Mountain People’s Warehouse and Cornucopia Natural Foods, becoming the first nationwide natural food distributor in 1996. It went public that same year, raising net proceeds of $35.5 million at $6.75 per share, after giving effect to a 2-for-1 stock split in 2004. Its stock trades around $40 a share, translating to a market cap just over $2.4 billion.

The company operates on a 52- or 53-week fiscal year (FY) ending the Saturday closest to July 31st. For the avoidance of doubt, the 52-week period ending August 2, 2025, is FY25.

Operating Segments

The company views the performance of its operations through three business segments: Natural; Conventional; and Retail.

Approximately half of UNFI’s top line comes from Natural, which consists of natural, organic, and specialty grocery and non-food products and services, including UNFI’s natural and organic brands and manufacturing operation. Servicing a $60 billion industry growing at mid-single digits that includes its largest customer (Amazon’s (AMZN) Whole Foods), it generated FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $442 million on net sales of $15.96 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $350 million on net sales of $14.87 billion in FY24, representing improvements of 26% and 7%, respectively.

Conventional consists of what the company deems “conventional” (processed) grocery and non-food products. Servicing a stagnate $30 billion industry, the segment generated FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $174 million on net sales of $13.48 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $219 million on net sales of $13.68 billion, representing declines of 21% and 1%, respectively.

Retail consists of UNFI’s 75 Cub Foods and Shoppers retail grocery stores, which offer both their own private label products as well as national and local brands. The unit provided FY25 Adj. EBITDA of $6 million on net sales of $2.34 billion versus Adj. EBITDA of $8 million on net sales of $2.44 billion in FY24, representing declines of 25% and 4%, respectively.

Putting it all together with corporate overhead, UNFI generated FY25 earnings of $0.71 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $552 million on net sales of $31.78 billion versus $0.14 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $518 million on net sales of $30.98 billion in FY24, representing gains of 407%, 7%, and 3%, respectively—9% and 5% at the Adj. EBITDA and net sales lines if FY24’s 53rd week is excluded.

Share Price Performance

Coming out of a poor half-decade triggered by the termination of its contract with Albertson’s (ACI) in 2015, further exacerbated by a regretful $2.9 billion acquisition of SuperValu stores in 2018—and later by pandemic uncertainty—UNFI's stock deteriorated from an all-time high of $83.91 per share to a two-decade low of $5.00 per share in March 2020. Emerging from the pandemic, the company enjoyed high (mid-14%) gross profits from food inflation (that peaked at 10.4% in 2022), generating a post-pandemic peak Adj. EBITDA of $829 million in FY22. Shares of UNFI reflected this dynamic, cresting at a six-year high of $57.89 in late 2021. However, as food inflation returned to the mid-2s, gross margins contracted 90 to 100 basis points over the ensuing two fiscal years (from mid-14% to mid-13%), while operating expense inflation continued, shrinking Adj. EBITDA to a six-year low of $518 million in FY24. Reflecting these headwinds, shares of UNFI fell 85% to $8.58 in April 2024, showcasing somewhat significant volatility for an otherwise dull, low-margin food distributor.

At that juncture, the company onboarded a new CFO, Giorgio Tarditi from General Electric (GE), joining CEO Sandy Douglas, who was appointed to his role in 2021 after a stint as the CEO of Staples. Shortly thereafter, UNFI entered into an eight-year extension to its distribution partnership with Whole Foods, and in January 2025, it realigned its commercial wholesale organization into the aforementioned natural and conventional segments, later embarking on a distribution optimization plan known as ‘lean management,’ which piggybacked on its $600 million investment in digitization that started in 2023—all in an effort to improve gross and operating margins. Aided by positive industry volume trends, shares of UNFI got off the mat and traded between $20 and $35 per share until its 4QFY25 financial report in late September 2025, in which it provided FY26 non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.90 versus a then consensus of $1.49, providing evidence that its efficiency initiatives were gaining traction while igniting a seven-day, 34% rally to $42.51 per share.

1QFY26 Financial Report & FY26 Guidance

Since that time, shares of UNFI have more or less round-tripped. That said, their 24% decline was not attributable to the company’s 1QFY26 financial report announced on December 2, 2025, when it posted non-GAAP EPS of $0.56 per share and Adj. EBITDA of $167 million on net sales of $7.84 billion versus $0.16 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $134 million on net sales of $7.87 billion in 1QFY25, reflecting improvements of 250% and 25% on a 0.4% decrease in net sales, the latter of which was a function of a 12% decline in conventional sales due to UNFI closing its Allentown, PA distribution center, offset by an 11% increase in its natural segment. Gross profit improved 20 basis points year-over-year to 13.4% as management’s efforts to decrease shrink (unaccounted loss of inventory due to theft, fraud, waste, inefficient processes, etc.) took increasing effect.

Furthermore, lean management practices were instituted at six additional distribution centers during the quarter, bringing the total to 34.

Management reiterated its FY26 guidance that sparked the late-September/early-October rally, maintaining earnings of $1.90 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $665 million on net sales of $31.8 billion, all based on range midpoints. During its Investor Day the following week, the company laid out its FY28 goals, including free cash flow of $300 million and Adj. EBITDA of $800 million on revenue of $33 billion, spurred mainly through efficiency gains. With UNFI’s strong showing at the bottom line, which beat expectations by $0.16, further suggesting that its initiatives were gaining significant momentum, the market rallied its stock 5% to $35.95 per share in the subsequent trading session, but that proved to be their highest close for the balance of 2025. Its shares are currently down 24% from their 2025 closing high set in October.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

In addition to the improvement at the earnings line, the company lowered its year-over-year net debt from $2.23 billion to $1.90 billion on November 1, 2025. Also aided by improvement in Adj. EBITDA, net leverage fell a full turn year-over-year to 3.2, with management eyeing 2.5 or below by YEFY26 and sub-2.0 by YEFY27. As for returning capital to shareholders, UNFI is focused on debt reduction. As such, it does not pay a dividend, and although it has a share repurchase authorization with $138 million remaining, it did not buy back any stock in FY25 or 1Q26.

Despite evidence of improvement at its bottom line, the company does not enjoy strong support from the Street, as can be seen above. That said, the last rating change was to the positive when BMO Capital upgraded UNFI from a hold to a buy in September 2025. On average, sell-side analysts expect UNFI to earn $2.18 per share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $31.9 billion in FY26, followed by $2.77 per share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $32.6 billion in FY27.

Board member James Pappas, representing the interests of JCP Investment Management, does not agree with Street consensus, purchasing 17,000 shares at an average price of $33.71 on January 2, 2026.

Verdict:

Although the gains from its optimization efforts appear small—20 basis points here or there—in a business with slim margins and ~$32 billion in sales, the impact on UNFI’s bottom line is tangible. The bet here is that they are also lasting, with the company’s FY28 goals very much attainable. That said, with a stated ‘goal’ for an FY28 Adj. EBITDA margin of 2.4%, UNFI’s stock is not a screaming value at just over 14 times FY27E non-GAAP EPS.

One month ago, UNFI was recommended as a small, covered call trade exclusively to Insider Forum members. The stock was trading at $34.00 a share at that time and has advanced nearly 20% since then. I would wait for the stock to fall back into the mid-$30s before I would reiterate that recommendation.

data.title: AIFU Inc.: Private Placement And Negative Earnings Have Disinterested Investors
data.description:
Intro

AIFU Inc. (AIFU), through its subsidiary, is a distributor of insurance products in China. AI is at the center of the company's operations, with the company not only offering a range of health and life insurance products but also offering underwriting services and claims adjusting services in its setup. Where AI is making its mark in the insurance industry is matching customer needs to adjoining products at scale. Data is the real currency of the information age, where AIFU is looking to use its proprietary AI to earn more revenue from each and every customer over time.

Nevertheless, the market has yet to buy into AIFU's vision of becoming a globally leading technological financial services platform, as we can clearly see from the technical chart below. In fact, the long-term chart makes for dismal reading, with the stock dropping from above $100 a share in early 2024 to approximately $2 a share at this juncture. The reported private placement last July of north of $31 million really seemed to put a dent into any possibility of a sustained rally last year. Therefore, the question becomes whether AIFU can finally turn the tables at its present valuation and market cap of approximately $200 million.

AIFU's Multi-Level Approach to Motivating Sales Agents

Similar to direct sales companies in the US, where agents get compensated for building big teams (downlines), AIFU uses a similar approach, which incentivizes workers. The problem with these types of compensation plans is that they rarely last for the full term, especially if AIFU were to expand the business outside Mainland China. AIFU will state that the comprehensive training it imparts ties sales agents to the company over time. However, history informs us that attrition rates remain well above average in compensation models that involve multi-level teams. Suffice it to say, given the lower commissions, especially on shorter-term insurance products, earning potential can remain low unless a high customer count and the right product work in tandem for the agent. To this point, given how AIFU's compensation plan works, team builders (more recruits) are the company's top priority over standard agents. This 'setup' from the outset can be seen as divisive, all things remaining equal.

Profitability Headwinds Will Remain Unless Robust Scale is Achieved

Therefore, when we take the analogy that our success (investor) has to be aligned with that sales agent on the last rung of the ladder, here is how we would boil everything down. When agents sell products, the insurance companies compensate AIFU, who obviously reward their chief team builders first (as this is where their priority lies) and finally our agent. Suffice it to say, look at how many parties are taking pieces of the margin pie before our sales agent grabs a foothold. You have the original insurance provider, then AIFU, then the agent's upline, so to speak, and finally himself.

Therefore, when we see margin compression trends in companies, it invariably puts extra pressure on achieving real top-line scale with respect to improving return on capital trends over time. This explains in part why AIFU is not only promoting a livelihood for its agents but also a business opportunity, as it appears to know margin suppression must be successfully offset with above-average scale. (More policies sold)

Chinese Regulator Lowering Commissions in Recent Times

In recent times, the Chinese regulator has stepped in to curb commissions that insurance companies pay out to the likes of brokers and insurance agencies alike. These actions (based on protecting the end customer) are due to the relatively low-interest rate environment, resulting in insurance companies raising their premiums in order to offset below-average investment returns on the back end. For the likes of AIFU, these regulatory actions (if they continue) will adversely affect the bottom line due to insurance companies being mandated by law to lower their commissions to the sellers of their products. In AIFU's most recent annual report, management pointed to further operational headwinds if present regulatory trends persist in China

In recent years, due to the decline in the interest rate of banking products in recent years, China’s insurance regulator has been implementing rules to adjust the pricing rate of life insurance products and has implemented a commission cap to the commission rate paid by insurance companies to insurance agencies and brokers like us to protect insurance companies from interest spread loss which has led to significant decline in our commission income and adversely affect our results of operations. If further cuts in commission rates are imposed by the regulator, our results of operations could be further adversely impacted.

Shares Trading Below Book Value

In the company's most recent reported numbers, total equity came in at $291.8 million. Therefore, given that the stock at present is trading with a market cap of $210.74 million, we can state that AIFU's trailing P/B multiple comes in at 0.72. This is a solid start, given the median P/B in this sector comes in at a much higher 1.35 and the significant dilution shares have undergone of late.

From a profitability standpoint, however, poor earnings performance in recent times, for reasons mentioned earlier, means AIFU is not profitable from a GAAP standpoint over the past four quarters. Furthermore, given the lack of any clear catalyst at this stage, we fear shares may need to go lower here over the near term before finally finding a floor. Therefore, despite AIFU's low book multiple, we believe the best rating is a soft sell until further notice.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we rate AIFU a 'Sell' at this juncture due to falling earnings, concerning technicals, and industry headwinds, which are affecting the company's insurance commissions meaningfully. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: TLT: Staying Bullish On Duration
data.description:
Today's article provides a fresh review of the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), a vehicle, which I held a bullish view on for most of 2025. Although it can be conceded that U.S. Treasury Bonds face a myriad of risks, it should also probably be acknowledged that implied returns have broadened considerably. In this updated take, I share why I think the TLT ETF could be a solid option at this time and how duration exposure can be managed.

Without further ado, let's get started.

Performance Update

TLT ETF has delivered around 3.19% in total return since I last covered it in July. Most of the return was derived from carry, as the price change only amounted to a 0.55% increase.

Figure two shows a summary of the vehicle's key risk-return metrics. The weighted-average Yield-to-Maturity is in line with the 4.85% reported in July last year. Furthermore, convexity remains notable at 3.30, effective duration is ~3 months lower, and the 30-day SEC yield is 4 basis points lower.

Bullish Case

YTMs Are High On A Relative-Basis, Coupons Are Compelling

I think U.S. yields are really competitive for longer-term positioning, as 20-year premiums are near their highest levels since the Great Financial Crisis. Although fears of fiscal dominance, USD debasement, and rich equity market valuations loom, I do not share the ex-U.S. sentiment echoed by many. I think the U.S.'s debt problem deserves attention. However, yields have acknowledged that and now provide investors with significantly higher yields than pre-pandemic (~+300 basis points). Moreover, it has to be recognized that real growth remains robust in the U.S., unemployment of 4.3% signals a stable labor market, and debt management is a problem across many developed markets, not just the United States.

On a country-relative basis, we've seen the U.S. maintain a positive sovereign spread relative to its relevant peers (excluding the UK), including Japan, France, and Germany – I discussed debt levels and economic indicators of each of these nations in an article last week (link here). I think Japanese bonds are becoming competitive. That being said, it has similar fiscal dominance and debt issues as the U.S., and therefore, I don't think a negative sovereign spread (Japan Yields - US Yields) would entice investors at this stage.

Inflation Re-Anchoring

U.S. CPI is due Friday the 13th and is expected to settle at 2.7%. Simultaneously, the Cleveland Federal Reserve now-casts 2.36% for January CPI and 2.59% for PCE. I think we might see some push-side inflation emerge via the recent unemployment numbers and a spike in crude oil. However, in general, I think the medium-term trajectory shows a sense of stabilization, which could detract a few basis points from the break-even rate, which currently stands at 2.32% for the 10-year (break-even prices both inflation expectations and uncertainty).

Furthermore, unemployment numbers have retraced back to the natural rate of ~4.3%. While that may signal stagflation, I believe it provides the U.S. Federal Reserve committee with a reason to halt rate cuts for a while. Why would the committee do that? In my view, as a seemingly pragmatic committee, they would think the labor market is still strong enough for us to focus on price stability. Ideally, we need to re-anchor inflation near 2% before it's too late. The labor data provides us with an opportunity to halt rate cuts and achieve our end goal of re-anchoring inflation – the Fed Futures market now prices a 94.1% probability of rates being held in March, versus the 89.1% in the previous week, showing alignment with the narrative that the Fed might hold rates.

You might be wondering, why would a halt on rates and strong unemployment numbers help TLT? In my view, the market might price the very short end higher for the time being, based on a slower-than-anticipated rate cuts cycle. That being said, I think the market will price inflation re-anchoring at the same time, with the thought that higher-for-longer rates will bring CPI and PCE down even further, in turn stabilizing breakevens. Furthermore, I think a halt on rate cuts could reduce the long-run neutral rate embedded in the 10-20 yields; a higher neutral rate is likely being priced at the short end as we speak due to stronger-than-anticipated labor, but if benchmark rates stay high, long-run neutral rate expectations could drop.

Am I dreaming? No, as illustrated by figure 8, the market actually priced a similar story on Wednesday after the labor data. Short-end higher, the rest of the curve lower.

In addition to the TLT ETF's YTM, its coupon base is solid, with its weighted average coupon sitting at 3.11%, which is around 40-50 basis points positive on a real basis. Lower rates will eventually decrease weighted-average coupons, but the effect is usually lagged vs. interest rates' influence on repricing. The TLT ETF would need to dispose of a significant amount of its current holdings for its coupon to settle lower.

Side Note: Coupon provides the carry component of YTM, and the rest is attributable to pull-to-par. I should also mention that the ETF has convexity; longer-duration bonds' convexity softens yield curve level-linked drawdowns and accelerates upside.

President Trump Doesn't Set Interest Rates, The Federal Reserve Committee Does

President Donald Trump has nominated Kevin Warsh to chair the U.S. Federal Reserve. The assumption is that Warsh will enforce a dovish policy stance, resulting in lower-than-justified effective rates. The Fed chair sets the agenda and controls public narrative, but the chair doesn't set rates, the committee votes. The committee has been divided lately; I doubt Warsh will walk in and convince everyone to slash rates and drive up nominal yields beyond acceptable levels. At most, I see a 25 basis point difference here (lower). The Federal Reserve committee is divided and filled with pragmatic leaders; an abrupt change in strategy remains far-fetched, and the yield curve probably won't steepen just because President Trump is talking down rates – if it does, it will likely be momentarily (due to noise).

Federal Reserve Demand Might Rise; Pensions and Insurers Might Engage In Secondary Purchases

The U.S. Federal Reserve announced an end to QT last year. I expect it to start buying long-dated Treasuries again. Expanding its balance sheet with a blend of RMBS and long-dated Treasuries seems prudent at this stage.

Banks might trade off and buy less at auctions if the Federal Reserve starts buying more, but given the USD's prominent role in funding liabilities and settlements, we can't really expect banks to repatriate too much. Moreover, as illustrated by figure 9, pensions were bought strongly in 2025, and we might see that continue in 2026 for liability-driven and regulatory-based purposes. Real rates have diminished at the shorter end, and longer-dated purchases make sense.

Risk Factors

Fiscal Concerns

Fiscal concerns can't be denied. Gross debt sits above 120%, and net government spending hasn't shown much of a decrease since the Trump administration arrived in Washington. In fact, foreign policy has evolved into a sphere where higher defense spending and talks of a Greenland acquisition might require higher government spending. Moreover, tax cuts could exacerbate fiscal concerns, especially if met by higher issuances.

Japan's 20s Have Become Competitive

Japan's 20s and 30s have risen sharply lately, due to a return of growth and inflation. In my view, we could see investors repatriate capital if yields converge with those of the United States.

Bond Risk-Return Profiles Are Concave

People love talking about convexity, and as I said earlier on, convexity can soften drawdowns while amplifying upside. However, don't be fooled; bond risk-return profiles are concave; convexity as a second-order effect of changes in yields won't change that fact. As illustrated by the next diagram, TLT ETF has a max drawdown of around 48.35%, which is higher than certain low-volatility stocks.

Mitigating Duration Risk

I often manage longer-end duration risk with gold. Keeping gold in a portfolio can hedge against duration events, especially if they are driven by inflationary uncertainty and fiscal risks. Figure 12 provides a correlation matrix between TLT and SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD). Ideally, one would need to factor in covariance and beta to size bets correctly.

Conclusion

I remain bullish about duration, especially if combined with gold as a hedge. Although fiscal concerns remain, I think yields compensate for risks and are unmatched by those of peer nations. Moreover, I believe institutional buying will pick up in 2026 and that inflation will re-anchor under a pragmatic Federal Reserve committee.

Bullish TLT for the first half of 2026.

data.title: Backfire Effect: Why Japanese PM Takaichi's Plan Could Make Things Worse
data.description:
A couple of months ago, I wrote an in-depth article regarding the Japanese monetary policy history. We can consider it a unique case among developed countries since it has kept interest rates near 0% for decades. This ultra-expansive approach led to the highest debt/GDP in the world and massive depreciation of the yen against major global currencies.

For decades, nobody was really worried about Japan’s finances; after all, it could refinance its debt at low interest rates, and the Bank of Japan was ready to buy every bond issuance. Actually, for the entire world, it was convenient to leave Japan in that condition. Through the carry trade, highly leveraged Japanese funds have flooded the financial markets and increased the demand for foreign assets. Everyone was benefiting from the carry trade, except for the yen's credibility.

However, that long-term balance is fading away, and Takaichi's recent election win might mark a breaking point.

Takaichi's resounding victory and commitments to uphold

Sanae Takaichi didn’t just win the snap election; she destroyed the opposition. The Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) reached 316 seats in the Lower House (House of Representatives), 118 more than the previous level and 2/3 of the total seats. Takaichi’s snap election was one of the greatest political moves in modern history: to see such a strong majority in the Japanese government, we should get back to 70 years ago. Thanks to this result, Takaichi’s plan can be implemented without any hiccups at least until 2028.

At this point an obvious question arises: why so many Japanese people were aligned to vote for Takaichi? The reason is pretty straightforward. The opposition proved to be very weak and insubstantial (that’s why Takaichi called the snap election), and Takaichi promised to revive the stagnant Japanese economy through massive fiscal stimulus packages.

Japan’s Prime Minister attributes the higher cost of living as the main cause of the economic deterioration, which is why she is committed to supporting the households’ expenses. First of all, she wants to suspend the 8% consumption tax on food for two years, and just this maneuver will cost as much as the entire education budget. There is also a strategic stance towards semiconductors and AI, where Takaichi quadrupled the funding in order to make Japan competitive from a technological standpoint.

Finally, the package aims at improving the national defense (2% GDP target) and making Japan less reliant on energy imports (87% of total usage). Over the short term, Takaichi wants to issue ¥11 trillion in energy subsidies as Japanese households complain about higher electricity prices, and over the long term she wants to focus on nuclear energy.

These are the promises made by Takaichi, and they all seem great. It doesn’t surprise me that she achieved such a strong consensus; it couldn’t have been otherwise. After all, your electricity bills will go down, you won’t pay the consumption tax, the country will improve its national defense, and it will return to threaten the U.S. technological lead. Everything seems great at first glance, but there is a “small” detail that I have hidden so far: the general account budget has reached 122.3 trillion yen, the highest ever recorded.

What lies beneath this optimism

Takaichi defends herself from the critics of overspending by highlighting some historical achievements, like a lower bond dependency ratio, the highest tax revenue ever, and a positive primary balance:

On the other hand, tax revenue is estimated at 83.7 trillion yen as we expect an improved economic climate combined with institutional factors such as the increase in the basic deduction, among other things. Consequently, the amount of newly issued government bonds is expected to be 29.6 trillion yen, marking the second consecutive year that the value of such bonds under an initial budget remains below the 30 trillion-yen threshold. The bond dependency ratio is also expected to decrease to 24.2%, falling further from 24.9% recorded at the time of the FY2025 initial budget, which already dropped below the 30% mark for the first time in 27 years. Additionally, we estimate the primary balance of the national government’s general account initial budget will turn positive for the first time in 28 years since the FY1998 budget.

In my opinion, these themes have led Japanese voters to think that the country could have handled such a massive fiscal budget, but the reality is quite different. First of all, the economic context has changed a lot over the last 10 years, and Japan can no longer print money without thinking too much about the consequences. The bond dependency ratio is still high despite the improvements, and issuing bonds worth 29.6 trillion yen is not a victory when your debt/GDP is way above 200%.

Takaichi’s vision, named “Sanaeconomics,” is even more expansive than previous “Abeconomics” (2012-2020), and the underlying reasons for such huge fiscal deficits are significantly different. That’s an important aspect to point out.

The purpose of Abeconomics was to inject liquidity into the system with the goal of stimulating Japanese consumption. In fact, as we all know, Japan from 2012 to 2020 suffered a period of deflation and declining GDP (in USD terms). Furthermore, Abeconomics started when the Debt/GDP was about 180%, and with ¥75, you could get $1. On the other hand, this is the context in which the Sanaeconomics will take place:

  • A Debt/GDP of 235%.
  • $1 is worth ~ ¥155 (USD:JPY).
  • The inflation rate has been higher than 2% for 44 months in a row.

In other words, the current Japan is significantly weaker compared to 2012, and it has already suffered a massive currency depreciation that led to an unexpected rise in the inflation rate and bond yields.

Regarding the expected primary balance surplus after 28 years, it is important to clarify that it doesn’t make the debt sustainability problem a walk in the park. This surplus means that the government’s revenue could compensate for the expenses; however, this figure doesn't include the maturing debt and interest payments.

If we take a look at Japan’s interest servicing, the situation seems very concerning. From 2016 to 2025, Japan was able to refinance the maturing debt at an average rate of 0.33%. Today bond yields are skyrocketing.

With a huge Debt/GDP ratio, refinancing the maturing debt over the next years might lead to the ultimate snowball effect on compound interest. According to a recent study by Morningstar, if the government refinances the maturing debt at 3%, interest servicing as a portion of government expenses could overcome 30% in 10 years. Basically, a third of the budget was wiped out by interest. And frankly, since bond yields are rising sharply, I wouldn’t be so sure that the 3% is the worst-case scenario.

The bond market knows the truth

Overall, Takaichi’s plan to revive the Japanese economy seems ambitious, and compared to previous PMs, she is taking on many more risks: Japan will either recover or collapse, no more middle ground. We are approaching a turning point.

Her goal is to let GDP growth outpace the debt growth, but if it fails, Japan will sink under its own debt. A default is unlikely since most of it is denominated in their own currency, but the yen might collapse even further. That’s the main risk.

At this point you might think, what’s the problem with a weaker yen? After all, Japanese companies will increase their exports. More revenues imply higher taxes to collect, and that’s what Takaichi wants. Well, let me introduce two aspects that support my concerns.

  • Japanese households' spending on food reached a 44-year high in 2025. Japan imports about 60% of its caloric intake (one of the highest levels among developed countries), and the U.S. is its main trading partner. Since the yen depreciated so much, food imports are getting increasingly expensive. The same reasoning applies to energy price increases, and it eventually reflects in a higher inflation rate.
  • Since Japan had, for decades, interest rates close to 0%, Japanese households had a strong incentive to choose a floating-rate mortgage. Currently, about 75% of them are paying a floating rate tied to the short-term prime rate. Needless to say, higher interest rates (it is already happening) will hurt Japanese households.

This is just the tip of the iceberg on why a too-weak currency tends to backfire, especially if you rely on energy and food imports. Takaichi wants to solve Japanese households’ financial distress by implementing a huge fiscal package, but in my opinion, this is just a short-term consolation. At the root of the problem, there is the deteriorated credibility of the yen, once considered a safe-haven asset. If the yen hadn’t depreciated so much, both food and energy prices wouldn’t be so high.

To really understand if Japan’s situation is getting any better, we should look at the long-term bond yields, and it doesn’t bode well.

The bond market knows the truth, much more than the stock market, and as of now, it has never trusted Takaichi’s vision. Quite the opposite, multiple times issued warnings following her intentions to print more money. This strategy has never worked in recent years, so why should it work now?

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta: Analyzing The Surge In Debt From $0 To $60 Billion In 5 Years
data.description:
I have been a Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) shareholder for almost a decade. For the best part of my holding period, Meta was debt-free. This is not the case anymore, with Meta ending 2025 with almost $60 billion in long-term debt ($58.74B). It is no secret that Meta’s growing debt pile has a lot to do with its ambitious AI plans. This growing debt pile has pushed Meta into a new business phase.

As investors, this is uncharted territory for us too, given that Meta always had a debt-free balance sheet. As a long-term shareholder, I wanted to evaluate what this debt pile means for the company and its shareholders. After a closer look at Meta’s capital allocation strategy, planned investments, and its net interest expense, I believe investors have nothing to fear about the company’s debt accumulation. On the contrary, the rising debt pile signifies a strategic capital allocation pivot that could reduce the WACC of investing in Meta.

Meta’s Goal Is To Keep Shareholder Distributions Alive Amid Negative FCF

Meta is in an aggressive investment phase. The company has guided for CapEx of between $115 billion and $135 billion for 2026, a massive step up from the $72 billion in CapEx Meta reported for 2025. Even in 2025, Meta’s CapEx almost doubled.

Exhibit 1: Meta’s capital expenditures

The bulk of these capital expenditures is directed toward AI infrastructure, including the construction of data centers, expanding cloud computing capacity, and purchasing AI chips. These investments are necessary to support the ambitious growth plans of Meta Superintelligence Labs.

Surging CapEx is expected to push Meta into negative FCF this year. That sounds quite bad on paper given that Meta has always been a cash cow. In 2025, Meta reported FCF of $43.6 billion. In my bear case scenario for FCF in 2026, I project negative FCF of around $8 billion.

Item

2025

2026 bear-case projection

Operating cash flow

$115.8 billion

$127.4 billion (10% YoY growth)

CapEx

$72.2 billion

$135 billion (high end of the management estimate)

Free cash flow

$43.6 billion

($7.6 billion)

Source: Company filings and author’s projections.

With FCF likely to reach a breakeven level or even negative in the worst-case scenario this year, Meta’s shareholder distributions might have faced a massive hurdle if not for the company tapping debt markets to boost its liquidity. For context, Meta spent almost $32 billion on buybacks and dividends in 2025. Without debt, Meta might have been forced to cut back on buybacks to focus only on dividends in the foreseeable future until CapEx normalizes. Given that Meta ended 2025 with $366 billion in assets, long-term debt of $58.7 billion brings the company’s debt-to-assets ratio to just 16%. This is quite manageable given the cash flow-rich nature of Meta’s business.

Given the substantial capital expenditures the company is likely to incur in the next couple of years, FCF is actually no longer a good indicator of Meta’s cash flow generation ability. For the next couple of years, I think investors need to look at Meta’s operating cash flows.

Meta’s Debt Load Is Less Alarming Than It Looks

Meta Platforms ended 2025 with almost $60 billion in debt. That sounds ominous. But upon closer inspection, this debt pile does not seem as ominous as it sounds.

According to company filings, in 2025, Meta incurred $1.16 billion in interest expenses. However, on the other hand, Meta generated $2.12 billion in interest income as well. The net positive impact is $958 million.

Exhibit 2: Meta’s interest income and expense

Meta is more like a bank at this point, generating higher interest income than expenses. There is a caveat, though. Meta completed a $30 billion bond sale in late October last year, so for most parts of 2025, interest expense was calculated on a total debt balance of around $30 billion, not $60 billion. The good news is that Meta will still break even from a net interest income perspective in 2026 despite the expected rise in interest expense.

I ran some numbers based on publicly available data to confirm whether Meta can maintain its net interest income cushion in 2026.

Based on SEC filings, Meta issued several bonds with varying maturities for an aggregate principal amount of $30 billion last October. The below list summarizes these bonds.

  • $4 billion of 4.200% Senior Notes due 2030
  • $4 billion of 4.600% Senior Notes due 2032
  • $6.5 billion of 4.875% Senior Notes due 2035
  • $4.5 billion of 5.500% Senior Notes due 2045
  • $6.5 billion of 5.625% Senior Notes due 2055
  • $4.5 billion of 5.750% Senior Notes due 2065.

The weighted average cost of this newly issued debt is 5.14%.

Based on this, Meta’s total interest expense will jump to around $2.75 billion in 2026. Unlike in 2025, Meta will only break even from a net interest income perspective even in the best-case scenario in 2026.

Although the recent bond sale has led to a massive increase in Meta’s indebtedness, the company can service this debt load easily.

Brace For More Debt Accumulation

Meta embracing debt did not happen by chance. This is a concerted effort by the management given how the business has matured enough to the extent where the focus has shifted to efficient capital allocation. From an accounting perspective, as many of you should know, debt is often much cheaper than equity. Commenting on Meta’s capital allocation strategy, CFO Susan Li said:

We will continue to look for opportunities to periodically supplement our strong operating cash flow with prudent amounts of cost-efficient external financing, which may lead us to eventually maintain a positive net debt balance.

By “a positive net debt balance,” Meta is thinking of permanently maintaining a long-term debt balance higher than its cash on hand. This represents a major strategic change, but one that I welcome given how it will lower the weighted average cost of capital.

Takeaway

Meta is no longer the debt-free tech giant it used to be. But this does not trouble me, as it does not threaten the long-term growth profile of the company. If anything, I would welcome a lower WACC for investing in Meta. I remain bullish on the company for a few reasons (which I have discussed in detail in my previous analyses), including the multi-billion-dollar opportunity for AI agents, the untapped market opportunity for core advertising growth outside of North America and Europe, and the monetization potential of WhatsApp.

data.title: American Superconductor: Story Still Has A Few Holes (Upgrade)
data.description:
To be honest, I have absolutely no idea what the market was doing with American Superconductor (AMSC) stock over the past eight months:

Investors sent the stock soaring into and out of fiscal first quarter earnings in late July. The report was excellent, certainly, but the company's own chief executive officer, Daniel McGahn, said on the Q1 call that the quarter was "near-perfect." That was true in terms of execution, but also in the external environment with "an ideal culmination of events" driving 34% gross margins and a rush order pulling revenue into the quarter.

Unsurprisingly, FQ2 results were less spectacular, and after ~tripling the stock across the Q1 release, the market halved it after the following quarter — even though those results looked simply normalized, rather than weak. That's been followed by an apparent shrug at Q3 numbers, with recent volatility perhaps driven more by trading in generative artificial intelligence/data center/power names than by a strong reaction to the print.

After all that drama, the stock is basically flat over the past year and up about 10% from where I panned it back in early June. Yet there is an argument that net/net, the results so far in fiscal 2025 (which ends March 2026) have been a plus for the long-term investment case in AMSC stock. I'm open to that argument and do see more reason for optimism than I did eight months ago, but a few concerns still keep me from flipping all the way positive.

The Numbers for AMSC Stock First

The headline numbers for AMSC stock can present a quite skewed picture of growth and profitability. The company has made two material acquisitions of late (NWL in August 2024 and Brazilian company Comtrafo in December 2025), which affect top-line comparisons. Tax benefits, including a huge $113 million gain on the release of a valuation allowance booked in Q3 FY25, skew earnings per share figures, as does relatively high-interest income (which still accounts for a large proportion of pre-tax profit).

But on an underlying basis, it's worth pointing out that AMSC stock still is priced for growth. At a current price of $33.20, the company's market capitalization is just shy of $1.5 billion. Net cash (including contingent consideration relating to the Comtrafo deal) is just under $100 million.

Pro forma operating income through nine months, per the 10-Q, is $19.5 million. That's a ~$26 million annualized run rate; AMSC's adjusted figures also add back stock-based compensation (~$16 million run rate), and there are likely some one-time costs in that GAAP pro forma figure as well. From here, current EBITDA earnings power is likely in the $50 million range (though it's worth noting the sell side is much less aggressive when looking to FY26 estimates).

That suggests EV/EBITDA around 28x, roughly speaking. A few million in EBITDA can move that multiple, but obviously it does incorporate solid growth going forward. EV/revenue, pro forma for Comtrafo, is about 4.2x.

On their face, neither multiple is necessarily prohibitive. Power/genAI plays are often getting bigger multiples; indeed, bigger players in the group are getting more aggressive multiples, even though their growth outlook may well be lighter. But, again, both incorporate relatively consistent mid-term growth.

The Bull Case for American Superconductor Stock

At the same time, AMSC stock quite obviously should be pricing in consistent growth. In both fiscal 2024 and year-to-date, pro forma revenue is up 12% (including a 22% print in Q3 FY25, though lumpiness at Comtrafo appears to explain a chunk of the acceleration). There are potential opportunities in data centers, where, per the Q3 call, AMSC just got its first win; in grid expansion and improvement across the country; in military and semiconductor end markets; and, in the wake of the Comtrafo deal, potentially from new products for electric utilities and new markets in Latin America.

There is a solid qualitative case here, clearly. McGahn summed it up well on the Q1 call, telling an analyst that, "I think the thesis is: 'Our time has kind of come here'." There are tailwinds across all four major markets (including an apparently large military win that should drive revenue down the line). Execution appears relatively solid of late, particularly for a company whose first 60 years or so were somewhat checkered (including one of the most infamous and damaging examples of intellectual property theft in history). Margins can improve; there is some room on capacity (though that would likely require a second shift at this point); and demand should be relatively strong for years to come. This is a company benefiting from several secular trends, whether it's semiconductor growth, grid improvement, military readiness, or (hopefully) data centers.

The Comtrafo acquisition in particular looks smart: it brings larger utility-scale transformers into the company's product line and also provides a beachhead for expansion into South America. (In FY24, 31% of revenue came from overseas, but in the grid business, the figure is far lower; likely closer to 20%.)

Again, the multiples here are high, but not prohibitively so given the outlook. There's a strong qualitative story here that matches those of stocks getting similar or higher multiples. The top-line growth this year has been particularly helpful to the bull case; as I wrote coming out of FY24 numbers, it wasn't actually clear that the company would be able to drive significant growth. As good as FY24 looked, FY23 was quite soft. And so it was at least possible that last year's growth was driven mostly by an easy comparison and some low-hanging fruit in terms of operational improvements. The fact that pro forma growth has been repeated so far in FY25 should provide some confidence in the longer-term case as well.

Breaking Down the Revenue

But there is a bit of a catch there. Looking more closely at revenue figures, the strength in the business quite obviously is uneven (these are year-to-date, pro forma figures calculated from AMSC filings; totals may not foot due to rounding):

Rev. Stream FY25 9M FY24 9M Growth % Total
AMSC Grid $125M $100M 25% 50%
AMSC Wind $35M $24M 46% 14%
NWL $48M $55M -13% 19%
Comtrafo $40M $43M -7% 16%
Total $248M $222M 12% 100%

From a high level, valuation does seem fairly reasonable. In terms of the individual parts, however, it gets a bit more problematic.

A current valuation at 4x-plus EV/revenue suggests ~13x EV/gross profit, given gross margins in the low 30s (and, at least so far, little expressed optimism from management that further significant expansion is on the way). And that is a reasonably big multiple. (It's comparable to a low double-digit revenue multiple for a software-as-a-service company, though to be fair, investors not that long ago were comfortable paying those kinds of prices.)

It's a bigger multiple in the context of the fact that, right now at least, basically half of the American Superconductor business looks really attractive. Wind is growing impressively, but that's basically a one-customer segment (India's Inox seems to have generated ~90% of segment sales so far this year) and so almost by definition is going to slow at some point. The fact that annualized revenue beyond Inox is literally about $5 million a year further suggests that the Wind revenue stream simply doesn't merit a really aggressive valuation.

Meanwhile, the pro forma figures suggest that both NWL and Comtrafo have posted declining revenue so far this fiscal year. It's possible Comtrafo's issue is just timing (Q3 was quite strong after a weak first half), but NWL's problems are relatively persistent. Sales for the business (again, using pro forma figures from AMSC filings) were down 1% in fiscal 2024 as well.

This issue existed coming into this fiscal year, but it seems even more pressing now. NWL sold for less than 1x revenue and remains ~19% of sales. Comtrafo went for about 3.7x, including the cost of land associated with factories, but might need to start proving the underlying business (as opposed to just the revenue synergies) is worth it. Wind is not a 4x business due to customer concentration alone.

But the other, obvious, concern is what this means for growth going forward as AMSC starts lapping inorganic additions. One-third of the business is not growing right now. The 14% from Wind almost by definition has to decelerate. Is legacy AMSC Grid really a consistent 20%-plus organic grower? It pretty much has to be for overall valuation and growth expectations to work here. And while it might be, it's difficult to yet have the confidence needed to bet that it will be, which at this point is what is needed to be long here.

Are We Worried About M&A Yet?

The other concern highlighted here is about whether the acquisition strategy — which per management commentary, seems likely to continue — is actually working. The AMSC stock price would mostly suggest yes, given that the stock is up ~60% since the NWL deal was announced, but broader secular tailwinds obviously have played a role as well.

To be sure, it is ludicrous to call either acquisition a mistake just yet. The NWL purchase price itself suggested American Superconductor management wasn't expecting torrid growth. It's possible the deal, which was expected to build out the military business, played a role in the recent contract win. (McGahn has been tight-lipped about the particulars of that deal, apparently for clearance reasons.) For Comtrafo, it's obviously way too early to tell.

But acquisitions obviously are always a risk, and with what looks like somewhat slow starts out of the gate, at the very least those risks aren't gone. It would be more comforting to see a bit better performance, and, again, this is a business trading at ~13x gross profit where one-third of its revenue, for now, is flatlining.

That can change in a hurry, certainly, and (as was the case last year) I don't see any reason to get in front of AMSC from the short side. But, even after solid performance so far in fiscal 2025, investors can only have real, long-term confidence in about half the business. At the right price, that's fine; for now, $33 still doesn't seem like the right price.
